title: Dog Seizures After Eating Compost: Immediate Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog seizing after eating compost? This is a high-priority emergency. Learn about mycotoxin poisoning and how to help your vet save your dog.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingesting compost is a life-threatening emergency for dogs because mold toxins can cause rapid-onset tremors and seizures. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your dog shows neurological symptoms after eating compost.

What happens when my dog eats compost and why is it toxic?

  • Dogs often ingest tremorgenic mycotoxins found in the molds of decaying organic matter like food scraps and garden waste.
  • These toxins directly attack the central nervous system, causing severe neurological symptoms.
  • The resulting tremors and seizures can be life-threatening without immediate professional treatment.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog ate compost?

  • Urgency Level: High. If your dog is experiencing seizures or severe tremors, contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.
  • This condition is a critical emergency that requires professional medical intervention.
  • Continuous muscle activity from seizures can cause hyperthermia (dangerous body temperature rise), leading to organ failure or death.

How can a photo or video help the veterinarian triage my dog?

  • If it is safe to do so, capture a short video of your dog's symptoms and a photo of the compost they ingested.
  • A video allows the veterinarian to distinguish between tremors, focal seizures, and grand mal seizures.
  • A photo helps identify specific molds or other hazards, such as coffee grounds or toxic plants, that require specific treatment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs presenting with seizures after potential compost ingestion, immediate treatment should be aimed at decontamination, seizure control, prevention of asphyxiation, and supportive care. Seizures should be controlled, and symptomatic animals stabilized before decontamination is attempted. If exposure is recent and clinical signs are not yet present, emesis should be induced with apomorphine (dogs only) at 0.03 mg/kg, IV, or 0.04 mg/kg, IM; or xylazine (dogs or cats) at 0.5-1 mg/kg, IV or IM. If emesis cannot be induced, gastric lavage should be performed. Animals already seizuring should be anesthetized first with pentobarbital, and an endotracheal tube passed before gastric lavage. After emesis or gastric lavage, activated charcoal should be administered at 2-3 g/kg with a cathartic such as magnesium sulfate at 250 mg/kg, PO, or sorbitol at 1-3 ml/kg, PO. Seizures should be controlled in small animals with pentobarbital, IV to effect, repeated as necessary, or methocarbamol at 100-200 mg/kg, IV, repeated as needed with a maximal dose of 330 mg/kg/day. Other medications such as glyceryl guaiacolate, diazepam, and xylazine have been used in dogs to control seizures with variable success. Propofol (3-6mg/kg, IV, or 0.1-0.6 mg/kg/min as a constant-rate IV infusion) or isoflurane inhalation anesthesia can also be tried if preceding measures are ineffective. Severely affected dogs should be intubated, and artificial respiration provided. IV fluids should be administered to force diuresis and maintain normal kidney function. Hyperthermia treatment should be given if necessary, and acid-base balance should be monitored and corrected.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3171)

faqs.question: How soon do symptoms of compost poisoning appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically appear within 30 minutes to three hours after ingestion, though they can sometimes manifest sooner depending on the amount eaten.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting in a dog that is seizing, tremoring, or acting disoriented, as this significantly increases the risk of aspiration pneumonia.

faqs.question: What is the typical treatment for mycotoxin poisoning?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians usually treat this with intravenous fluids, muscle relaxants, anti-seizure medications, and active cooling measures to stabilize the dog's temperature.
